blog_text:
9 Examples of Real Zombies in the Animal World

by Simon Currie, Ph.D.

by Simon Currie, Ph.D.

Everyone knows that zombies aren’t real, just the stuff of fictional Halloween movies, right?

Not so fast! There are numerous examples of parasites such as fungi, wasps and other organisms turning animals into zombies.

What exactly do we mean by zombies? Well, these parasites hijack the animals’ decision making ability and influence them to perform actions that support the parasites’ lifecycle. Unlike symbiotic relationships where both organisms cooperate and flourish, these interactions are to the detriment of the host animal and usually end in their death … spooky!

In this article we’ll cover some real-life examples of parasites and how they turn animals into zombies.

Have you ever been to a rodeo and seen a cowboy riding a bucking horse? That’s kind of like what the jewel wasp does to a cockroach, except the wasp has an extra trick that makes the ride a whole lot smoother. First, they sting the cockroach on the top of the head and inject a venomous, mind-controlling cocktail into their brain (Gal and Libersat, 2010). After the venom’s effect has settled in, the wasp chomps off one of the cockroach’s antennae, bites the remaining antennae stump, and guides the cockroach to a prepared burrow.

This isn’t just some innocuous fieldtrip; however, the jewel wasp has a specific purpose in mind for zombifying the cockroach. The wasp lays an egg inside the cockroach’s back. Then, the jewel wasp finds objects to block the cockroach in the burrow so that another predator won’t find and eat the cockroach incubator. Later, when the jewel wasp eggs hatch, the cockroach provides the developing wasp larvae with a nice meaty snack while they grow and eventually emerge leaving the cockroach carcass behind.

The injection of the mind-controlling cocktail is the vital step in this interaction and is teaching scientists about neurological networks (Libersat and Gal, 2014). The key here is that this cocktail doesn’t paralyze the cockroach, otherwise it would make the task of dragging the cockroach to the burrow a lot more strenuous! Rather, these mind-controlling substances induce a lethargic state in the cockroach and reduce their intrinsic motivation to initiate movement, thereby allowing the wasp to walk the cockroach, like a dog on a leash, to its eventual demise.

Cockroaches aren’t the only creatures that wasps coerce into doing their bidding. Wasps also target spiders! Ichneumonidae wasps sting Tetragnathidae spiders and induce a temporary paralysis of about 15 minutes during which the wasp glues an egg to the spider’s abdomen (Eberhard, 2000).

After the paralysis wears off, the spider resumes normal behavior and builds its regular webs for the next 7-14 days while the wasp egg hatches and the larva grows by drinking the spider’s hemolymph, which is the invertebrate equivalent of blood.

On the night that the developing wasp larvae will kill the spider, it chemically induces the spider to deviate from its normal web building style. The spider will repeat the first step of building its web, over and over, without proceeding to any of the subsequent steps. The end result is that instead of spinning a normal web, the spider spins a tiny web that provides silk starting material that the larva can easily use to spin its cocoon.

After the spider has spun this deviant web, the larva kills and eats the spider, then spins its silk cocoon to finish developing for another eleven days. Although it’s not known exactly how the wasp larva chemically controls the spider’s behavior, this is considered one of the most subtle perturbances of host behavior by just slightly modifying how it builds its web to generate cocoon starting material instead of a regular web (Eberhard, 2000).

So far, we’ve heard about two instances where killing the host provides food for the developing parasitic larvae to grow and complete its lifecycle. But sometimes, controlling a host’s mind is all about getting back to the right place so that the parasite can complete its lifecycle.

California killifish and their parasitic flatworms (Euhaplorchis californiensis), are an example of this relationship. The flatworms can live inside the killifish, but they cannot reproduce there. So, they influence the fish’s behavior to get back into birds where they are able to reproduce.

The flatworms adjust the killifish’s brain chemistry, altering serotonin and dopamine metabolism to encourage the fish to engage in riskier behavior (Shaw et al, 2009). This change means that the fish swim closer to the water’s surface where they are easily snatched up and eaten by avian predators. Back inside the birds, the flatworm reproduces and forms thousands of cysts, allowing their lifecycle to continue. Who says you can’t go home? (You just might need the help of a fish to get there.)

Another example of parasites influencing a host’s behavior involves the single-celled parasite Toxoplasma gondii, and gray wolves. T. gondii can live in the intestines of wolves, but only reproduce in felines.

Researchers suspect that wolves are exposed to T. gondii after eating the feces of infected cougars. Infected wolves were more likely to display risky behavior such as leaving their pack to form a new one, or asserting themselves as pack leaders (Meyer et al, 2022).

Collectively, these behaviors increase the range of territory that wolves from infected packs travel, increasing the likelihood for further bidirectional transmission from a wolf to a cougar and from cougars back into wolves.

Keep in mind that leaving their pack or challenging the existing pack leader are both risky behaviors that put the individual wolf at increased likelihood of death. So, this is another example where a parasite skews a host’s decision making to prioritize the parasitic lifecycle over the host’s health.

T. gondii can also infect mice, but, as we discussed previously, it only reproduces inside felines. T. gondii influences mice in at least two ways that make it more likely for them to get eaten by cats – an event that allows the parasite to reproduce once back inside the cats.

First, the parasite increases testosterone production in mice, making them less afraid of cats (Ingram et al, 2013). While sometimes it’s important to be brave and less afraid in life, in this case it is much more beneficial for the mice to be appropriately afraid of cats!

Second, mice are naturally averse to cat urine. The scent of cat urine makes them avoid that area, making them less likely to come into contact with a cat and be eaten. T. gondii reverses this natural reaction and instead makes mice attracted to the scent of cat urine (House et al, 2011). This makes the infected mouse more likely to come into contact with a cat, and of functional importance, in contact with the cat’s mouth!

Interestingly, this behavior is also observed in T. gondii-infected chimpanzees who become attracted to the urine of leopards, which are a chimpanzee’s only natural predator (Poirotte et al, 2016).

Have you read or seen The Last of Us (Druckmann & Staley, 2023)? In that fictional story, fungi colonize humans, turning them into zombies that bite one another to spread the fungal infection. The plot isn’t totally implausible as there are fungal infections that parasitize insects and animals for their benefit.

In fact, the actual fungus from that story, Ophiocordyceps unilateralis, is parasitic in insects. For example, this fungus infects carpenter ants and directs them to leave their colony and move towards high ground by following light. Usually this means that the carpenter ant climbs a tree or otherwise reaches a high point.

Then the fungus produces a spore that projects out of the ant’s head, killing the insect and releasing spores. Since the ant is at a high point, the stalk can distribute spores across a broader area, thereby more efficiently promoting fungal spread.

A different type of fungus, Massoospora cicadina attacks cicadas emerging from hibernation and deteriorates their abdomen and butt, leaving a clump of yellow spores in their place (Cooley et al, 2018). The fungus also ramps up the cicada’s sex drive which facilitates the transfer of fungal spores to other cicadas, and the fungal life cycle continues in that manner. Apparently, the yellow rear-end does repel potential cicada suitors!

It’s not just cicadas whose libido is ramped up by fungi, but frogs too. The fungus Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis infects multiple types of frogs and increases their likelihood of finding a mate.

Infected male common mist (Litoria rheocola) frogs called more for a female mate during the night compared to uninfected frogs. Their efforts paid off, as on average the infected frogs were more successful at finding a mate (Greenspan et al, 2016).

Other researchers found that male Japanese tree frogs (Hyla japonica) infected with the same fungus called for a mate more rapidly and produced longer mating calls compared to uninfected frogs. Again, the infected male frog’s enhanced efforts paid off because they more frequently mated compared to their uninfected counterparts (An & Waldman, 2016).

For both of these types of frogs, it is thought that enhanced reproduction rates are coupled with increased fungal transmission, which is why the fungus activates these frogs’ nighttime activities.

Our last example is of a barnacle (Sacculina carcina) tricking a female crab into taking care of it as she would her own babies.

First, the barnacle finds the soft spot near joints on the underbelly of a crab where she normally carries her babies. The barnacle then sheds her shell and burrows into the crab, projecting tendrils throughout the crab to steal nutrients and deliver commands to the pseudo-maternal caretaker. The barnacle hijacks the maternal instincts of the crab such that the crab will protect, groom, and flush the barnacle with oxygenated water, just as it would do for its own offspring (Powell & Rowley, 2008).

Now that the female barnacle is well situated, it bores a hole through the crab’s shell and invites a male barnacle to come mate, and then continues using the crab’s forced goodwill to care for its own offspring. Talk about a toxic relationship!

While the exact details of each of these zombie examples are known to differing extents, what is clear in all of the cases is that the control the parasite exerts over the host is executed through chemical communication that alters brain chemistry.

Hopefully, you would notice if you had a jewel wasp on your back tugging at your hair, or a barnacle burrowed into your underbelly. In the absence of any such obvious hitchhikers, it may still be worth examining if the external influences in your life – such as the food, substances, or media that you consume – are adjusting your brain chemistry and causing you to act against your best interests, also known as turning you into a zombie!

metadata.summary: The blog explores real-life examples of parasites that can control the behavior of host animals, effectively turning them into 'zombies'. It covers how these parasites, like jewel wasps and fungi, manipulate their hosts to aid the parasites' life cycles, often at the expense of the host's survival.

currency:
## Currency Analysis

**1. Publication/Update Date**
The publication or update date is not visible in the provided text. Without this information, it is unclear if the timing is appropriate for the topic.

**2. Currency for the Field**
The topic relates to biological interactions, which do not change as rapidly as fields like technology or medicine. However, the use of studies from 2010 and 2014 suggests the content may not represent the latest research.

**3. Links and References**
The references are to academic studies from 2000, 2010, and 2014. There are no visible links or indications of whether these references are current or functional.

**4. Maintenance**
There are no signs in the text of updates, revision history, or recent comments indicating active maintenance.

**5. Obsolescence Risk**
The text relies on older studies, and while the biological processes described may still be valid, there is a risk of missing more recent advancements or discoveries in this area.
accuracy_text:
## Accuracy Analysis

**1. Sources and Evidence**
The blog post provides some academic citations as evidence. It mentions "Gal and Libersat, 2010" for the behavior of the jewel wasp with cockroaches and "Libersat and Gal, 2014" for neurological studies on the wasp's venom. Additionally, it references "Eberhard, 2000" concerning the Ichneumonidae wasps and Tetragnathidae spiders.

**2. Errors, Contradictions, and Inconsistencies**
There are no obvious factual errors, contradictions, or logical inconsistencies detected in the section provided. The explanations align logically with the examples given, following a coherent narrative about parasitic behaviors.

**3. Facts vs. Opinions**
The author does not clearly distinguish between facts and opinions through language such as "I believe" or "in my view." The article primarily presents factual descriptions bolstered by references, without evident use of opinion or hedging language.

**4. Verifiability**
The main claims about the behavior of the jewel wasp and Ichneumonidae wasps can likely be verified through reputable scientific studies and peer-reviewed research, as the claims are supported by specific citations. Checking these references in academic databases would confirm the accuracy of the described phenomena.

facts.fact: The jewel wasp stings a cockroach on the head and injects a venomous, mind-controlling cocktail into its brain, as studied by Gal and Libersat in 2010.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** The jewel wasp stings a cockroach on the head and injects a venomous, mind-controlling cocktail into its brain, as studied by Gal and Libersat in 2010.

**Verdict:** TRUE

**Justification:** Researchers Ram Gal and Frederic Libersat confirmed that the parasitoid jewel wasp (Ampulex compressa) delivers a cocktail of neurotoxins via a precise sting into the cockroach’s head—specifically targeting brain ganglia such as the sub‑esophageal ganglion (SEG) and supra‑esophageal ganglion. This injection initiates behavioral changes in the cockroach, including reduced locomotion and a "zombified" state. Their PLOS ONE study in April 2010 details these findings, showing that venom injection into the SEG decreases spontaneous walking and neuronal activity, rendering the cockroach unable to self-initiate movement ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20383324/?utm_source=openai)).

**References:**
- A wasp manipulates neuronal activity in the sub‑esophageal ganglion to decrease the drive for walking in its cockroach prey – Ram Gal & Frederic Libersat (PLoS ONE, 2010) ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20383324/?utm_source=openai))  
- What can parasitoid wasps teach us about decision‑making in insects? – Journal of Experimental Biology (reviews the 2010 Gal & Libersat work) ([journals.biologists.com](https://journals.biologists.com/jeb/article/216/1/47/11351/What-can-parasitoid-wasps-teach-us-about-decision?utm_source=openai))

facts.fact: The jewel wasp bites off one of the cockroach’s antennae and uses the remaining stump to guide it to a burrow where it lays an egg inside the cockroach’s back.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** The jewel wasp bites off one of the cockroach’s antennae and uses the remaining stump to guide it to a burrow where it lays an egg inside the cockroach’s back.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:**
The claim is largely accurate with respect to the jewel wasp (Ampulex compressa) biting off the cockroach’s antennae and using the stumps to lead it like a leash into a burrow, where it proceeds to lay an egg on the cockroach. Multiple credible sources confirm this behavior ([orkin.com](https://www.orkin.com/pests/stinging-pests/wasps/emerald-cockroach-wasp?utm_source=openai)). However, the egg is laid **on the cockroach’s leg or abdomen**, not "inside the cockroach’s back" as the claim suggests. The larva later enters the cockroach’s body by chewing through the cuticle to develop internally, but initial oviposition is external ([orkin.com](https://www.orkin.com/pests/stinging-pests/wasps/emerald-cockroach-wasp?utm_source=openai)).

**References:**
- How To Identify & Get Rid Of Emerald Cockroach Wasps (Orkin)  
- On predatory wasps and zombie cockroaches: Investigations ... (PMC/NCBI)  
- University of California research summary on Ampulex compressa behavior

facts.fact: The jewel wasp blocks the burrow to protect the cockroach, which acts as an incubator for the wasp's larvae, eventually leading to the cockroach's death.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** The jewel wasp blocks the burrow to protect the cockroach, which acts as an incubator for the wasp's larvae, eventually leading to the cockroach's death.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:**  
Evidence supports that the jewel wasp (Ampulex compressa) drags a zombified cockroach into a burrow, lays an egg on it, and then seals the burrow entrance to protect the developing larva and egg from predators and environmental hazards ([insecthive.com](https://insecthive.com/why-this-wasp-mummifies-cockroaches-1-4440/?utm_source=openai)). The cockroach indeed serves as a living incubator and food source; the larva eventually consumes and kills it ([insecthive.com](https://insecthive.com/why-this-wasp-mummifies-cockroaches-1-4440/?utm_source=openai)).

However, the notion that the burrow is blocked specifically to "protect the cockroach" is misleading. The primary purpose of sealing the burrow is to safeguard the egg and larva, not to preserve the cockroach itself ([wired.com](https://www.wired.com/2014/02/absurd-creature-of-the-week-jewel-wasp/?utm_source=openai)).

**References:**  
- "Why This Wasp Mummifies Cockroaches – Insect Hive" — seals burrow to protect egg and developing larva ([insecthive.com](https://insecthive.com/why-this-wasp-mummifies-cockroaches-1-4440/?utm_source=openai))  
- "Absurd Creature of the Week: The Wasp That Enslaves Cockroaches…" — sealing protects the egg and larva more than preventing escape ([wired.com](https://www.wired.com/2014/02/absurd-creature-of-the-week-jewel-wasp/?utm_source=openai))  
- "Jewel Wasp: Complete Guide…" — female seals burrow after laying egg; cockroach remains living food source ([waspexpert.com](https://www.waspexpert.com/posts/jewel-wasp-complete-guide-emerald-cockroach-wasp?utm_source=openai))

purpose.tone: The tone is primarily objective with descriptive elements. The text informs readers about a biological phenomenon with statements such as “these parasites hijack the animals’ decision making ability,” which are factual and informative but also presented in an engaging manner.
purpose.style: The writing style is conversational. This is evident in the use of rhetorical questions like “Everyone knows that zombies aren’t real, just the stuff of fictional Halloween movies, right?” and casual language such as “...a nice meaty snack while they grow...”
purpose.bias: None detected. The content does not show bias toward or against any social, political, or ideological groups. It neutrally presents information about biological interactions.
purpose.sentiment: The overall sentiment is neutral. The text provides factual information about parasites and their behavior without expressing strong emotions or opinions.
purpose.hate: None detected. The language is scientific and devoid of any vulgar, hateful, discriminatory, or offensive terms.
justifications: The article uses factual language and scientific references, such as “Gal and Libersat, 2010,” to support its claims about biology.
The conversational tone is illustrated by phrases like “Not so fast!” and analogies, such as comparing the wasp’s actions to a cowboy riding a horse.
There are no indications of bias or discrimination; the topic is explored from a purely scientific perspective.
No ads, sponsorship disclosures, or affiliate links are mentioned in the content provided.

author_authority.positive_mentions: Simon Currie, Ph.D. is a contributor of multiple technical articles on the GoldBio website covering topics such as protein purification (e.g., glutathione function, biotinylated molecule purification, computational protein design, verifying plasmids, IPTG stock solution), demonstrating expertise in molecular biology and biochemical techniques — all authored under his byline as “by Simon Currie, Ph.D.” ([goldbio.com](https://www.goldbio.com/articles/article/beyond-ph-7-biologically-relevant-ph-in-diverse-environments?utm_source=openai))
author_authority.negative_mentions: No controversies, legal issues, or negative media coverage were found regarding Simon Currie, Ph.D. affiliated with GoldBio in the publicly available sources searched.
author_authority.sources:
• GoldBio articles authored by Simon Currie, Ph.D. (multiple) — credible professional content from company website ([goldbio.com](https://www.goldbio.com/articles/article/beyond-ph-7-biologically-relevant-ph-in-diverse-environments?utm_source=openai))
• Academic event listing (SLU chalk talk) confirming his position as a postdoctoral researcher, though affiliation appears with UT Southwestern Medical Center on event page — indicating academic standing ([biochem.slu.edu](https://biochem.slu.edu/event/currie-chalktalk/?utm_source=openai))
author_authority.summary: Simon Currie, Ph.D., associated with GoldBio, is prominently featured as the author of numerous educational and technical articles on the GoldBio website, spanning key areas of molecular biology and biochemistry. These contributions establish him as a knowledgeable scientific communicator with hands-on experience in protein chemistry, molecular labeling, genetic tools, and experimental protocols. He also appears as a postdoctoral researcher in an academic seminar event coordinated by Saint Louis University’s Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, hosted by UT Southwestern Medical Center, indicating active involvement in academic research circles. There is no indication of controversies or negative reports in accessible public records or media. Based on available evidence, his reputation in professional and academic contexts is positive and credible, with demonstrated expertise in biochemical research and science communication.
author_authority.justification: The appraisal relies on directly sourced professional content authored by Simon Currie, Ph.D., on an official company platform (GoldBio), showcasing his domain expertise through multiple explanatory articles. The academic chalk talk listing further supports his active engagement in scientific research. No conflicting or negative information was found across news, public records, or other platforms searched, indicating a clean professional reputation.

publisher_authority.ownership_and_funding: GoldBio, officially Gold Biotechnology, Inc., is a privately held company founded around 1986 by Paul Gold, and is headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. It remains privately owned and operated, with no apparent major external investors or publicly listed funding sources. This structure suggests a high degree of independence from large corporate or political influence. (Sources: company “About Us” page ([goldbio.com](https://www.goldbio.com/pages/about-goldbio?utm_source=openai)); Crunchbase profile ([crunchbase.com](https://www.crunchbase.com/organization/goldbio?utm_source=openai)).)
publisher_authority.editorial_standards: GoldBio is primarily a supplier of biochemical reagents rather than a news or media publisher. Its website contains product descriptions, technical articles (e.g., “What is Plant Genetic Transformation?” by Adriana Gallego, Ph.D. ([goldbio.com](https://www.goldbio.com/articles/article/What-Plant-genetic-transformation?utm_source=openai))), and an FAQ. While the company communicates product information and protocols, there is no publicly documented editorial policy, fact‑checking framework, corrections policy, or clear distinction between opinion and informational content. It appears to focus on technical communication to support researchers, but lacks formal journalism standards.
publisher_authority.political_bias: As a scientific products supplier, GoldBio does not engage in political content. There is no indication or assessment from media‑bias organizations (e.g., Media Bias/Fact Check, AllSides) evaluating any political orientation, since the company does not operate in the news media space.
publisher_authority.factual_reliability: GoldBio’s materials appear to be accurate and reliable within its domain of biochemical products. Articles are authored by experts (e.g., Ph.D. scientists ([goldbio.com](https://www.goldbio.com/articles/article/What-Plant-genetic-transformation?utm_source=openai))) and the company uses partnerships such as Bioz Prime Badges to display how frequently its products are cited in peer‑reviewed research ([biospace.com](https://www.biospace.com/press-releases/gold-biotechnology-enhances-visibility-and-engagement-through-bioz-partnership?utm_source=openai)). Moreover, user feedback from scientific forums (e.g., Reddit’s r/labrats) indicates that researchers find GoldBio products quality‑comparable to others at more affordable prices ([reddit.com](https://www.reddit.com/r/labrats/comments/esf0sd?utm_source=openai)). There are no notable reports of misinformation or retractions related to the company’s technical claims.
publisher_authority.public_perception: Among scientists and users, GoldBio appears to have a positive reputation. Customers on Reddit describe their reagents as high‑quality and affordable, and highlight responsive customer service ([reddit.com](https://www.reddit.com/r/labrats/comments/esf0sd?utm_source=openai)). Online consumer review site Knoji gives GoldBio an average rating of 3.9 out of 5 based on 17 reviews as of early March 2026 ([goldbio.knoji.com](https://goldbio.knoji.com/?utm_source=openai)). The company has also received recognition within research communities through partnerships like the Bioz Prime Badges ([biospace.com](https://www.biospace.com/press-releases/gold-biotechnology-enhances-visibility-and-engagement-through-bioz-partnership?utm_source=openai)). As a supplier rather than a journalistic entity, GoldBio does not feature in awards like Pulitzer or Peabody, nor in academic credibility disputes.
publisher_authority.summary:
Gold Biotechnology (GoldBio) is a privately owned, small‑to‑mid‑sized life‑science reagent supplier founded in the mid‑1980s, operating from St. Louis. It appears financially independent, with no reliance on external investors or political affiliations. It communicates product information and technical guidance, usually authored by credentialed scientists, but does not follow formal editorial or journalistic standards. There is no political orientation, as its output is focused solely on scientific and technical content rather than news or commentary. Its factual reliability is reinforced by product adoption in peer-reviewed research and positive user feedback regarding product quality and affordability. Public perception among research users is favorable, emphasizing value and quality.

Credibility Rating: **Medium to High**. As a specialized supplier, GoldBio is credible within its own domain of biochemical reagents, with strong product reputation and transparency in ownership. However, because it does not engage in editorial journalism, standard media‑credibility frameworks are not applicable. Consumers and researchers should assess it accordingly—as a reliable products and protocol provider rather than a media publisher.

Search URL: https://www.google.com/search?q=GoldBio

raw_metadata.title: 9 Examples of Real Zombies in the Animal World
raw_metadata.author: Simon Currie; Ph D
raw_metadata.description: Everyone knows that zombies aren’t real, just the stuff of fictional Halloween movies, right? Not so fast! There are numerous examples of parasites such as fungi, wasps and other organisms turning animals into zombies. What exactly do we mean by zombies? Well, these parasites hijack the animals’ decision making ability and influence them to perform actions that support the parasites’ lifecycle. Unlike symbiotic relationships where both organisms cooperate and flourish, these interactions are to the detriment of the host animal and usually end in their death … spooky! In this article we’ll cover some real-life examples of parasites and how they turn animals into zombies. Jewel Wasps and Cockroaches Have you ever been to a rodeo and seen a cowboy riding a bucking horse? That’s kind of like what the jewel wasp does to a cockroach, except the wasp has an extra trick that makes the ride a whole lot smoother. First, they sting the cockroach on the top of the head and inject a venomous, mind-controlling cocktail into their brain (Gal and Libersat, 2010). After the venom’s effect has settled in, the wasp chomps off one of the cockroach’s antennae, bites the remaining antennae stump, and guides the cockroach to a prepared burrow. This isn’t just some innocuous fieldtrip; however, the jewel wasp has a specific purpose in mind for zombifying the cockroach. The wasp lays an egg inside the cockroach’s back. Then, the jewel wasp finds objects to block the cockroach in the burrow so that another predator won’t find and eat the cockroach incubator. Later, when the jewel wasp eggs hatch, the cockroach provides the developing wasp larvae with a nice meaty snack while they grow and eventually emerge leaving the cockroach carcass behind. The injection of the mind-controlling cocktail is the vital step in this interaction and is teaching scientists about neurological networks (Libersat and Gal, 2014). The key here is that this cocktail doesn’t paralyze the cockroach, otherwise it would make the task of dragging the cockroach to the burrow a lot more strenuous! Rather, these mind-controlling substances induce a lethargic state in the cockroach and reduce their intrinsic motivation to initiate movement, thereby allowing the wasp to walk the cockroach, like a dog on a leash, to its eventual demise. Wasps and Spiders Cockroaches aren’t the only creatures that wasps coerce into doing their bidding. Wasps also target spiders! Ichneumonidae wasps sting Tetragnathidae spiders and induce a temporary paralysis of about 15 minutes during which the wasp glues an egg to the spider’s abdomen (Eberhard, 2000). After the paralysis wears off, the spider resumes normal behavior and builds its regular webs for the next 7-14 days while the wasp egg hatches and the larva grows by drinking the spider’s hemolymph, which is the invertebrate equivalent of blood. On the night that the developing wasp larvae will kill the spider, it chemically induces the spider to deviate from its normal web building style. The spider will repeat the first step of building its web, over and over, without proceeding to any of the subsequent steps. The end result is that instead of spinning a normal web, the spider spins a tiny web that provides silk starting material that the larva can easily use to spin its cocoon. After the spider has spun this deviant web, the larva kills and eats the spider, then spins its silk cocoon to finish developing for another eleven days. Although it’s not known exactly how the wasp larva chemically controls the spider’s behavior, this is considered one of the most subtle perturbances of host behavior by just slightly modifying how it builds its web to generate cocoon starting material instead of a regular web (Eberhard, 2000). Flatworms and California Killifish So far, we’ve heard about two instances where killing the host provides food for the developing parasitic larvae to grow and complete its lifecycle. But sometimes, controlling a host’s mind is all about getting back to the right place so that the parasite can complete its lifecycle. California killifish and their parasitic flatworms (Euhaplorchis californiensis), are an example of this relationship. The flatworms can live inside the killifish, but they cannot reproduce there. So, they influence the fish’s behavior to get back into birds where they are able to reproduce. The flatworms adjust the killifish’s brain chemistry, altering serotonin and dopamine metabolism to encourage the fish to engage in riskier behavior (Shaw et al, 2009). This change means that the fish swim closer to the water’s surface where they are easily snatched up and eaten by avian predators. Back inside the birds, the flatworm reproduces and forms thousands of cysts, allowing their lifecycle to continue. Who says you can’t go home? (You just might need the help of a fish to get there.) Intestinal Parasites and Wolves Another example of parasites influencing a host’s behavior involves the single-celled parasite Toxoplasma gondii, and gray wolves. T. gondii can live in the intestines of wolves, but only reproduce in felines. Researchers suspect that wolves are exposed to T. gondii after eating the feces of infected cougars. Infected wolves were more likely to display risky behavior such as leaving their pack to form a new one, or asserting themselves as pack leaders (Meyer et al, 2022). Collectively, these behaviors increase the range of territory that wolves from infected packs travel, increasing the likelihood for further bidirectional transmission from a wolf to a cougar and from cougars back into wolves. Keep in mind that leaving their pack or challenging the existing pack leader are both risky behaviors that put the individual wolf at increased likelihood of death. So, this is another example where a parasite skews a host’s decision making to prioritize the parasitic lifecycle over the host’s health. Intestinal Parasites and Mice T. gondii can also infect mice, but, as we discussed previously, it only reproduces inside felines. T. gondii influences mice in at least two ways that make it more likely for them to get eaten by cats – an event that allows the parasite to reproduce once back inside the cats. First, the parasite increases testosterone production in mice, making them less afraid of cats (Ingram et al, 2013). While sometimes it’s important to be brave and less afraid in life, in this case it is much more beneficial for the mice to be appropriately afraid of cats! Second, mice are naturally averse to cat urine. The scent of cat urine makes them avoid that area, making them less likely to come into contact with a cat and be eaten. T. gondii reverses this natural reaction and instead makes mice attracted to the scent of cat urine (House et al, 2011). This makes the infected mouse more likely to come into contact with a cat, and of functional importance, in contact with the cat’s mouth! Interestingly, this behavior is also observed in T. gondii-infected chimpanzees who become attracted to the urine of leopards, which are a chimpanzee’s only natural predator (Poirotte et al, 2016). Fungi and Ants Have you read or seen The Last of Us (Druckmann & Staley, 2023)? In that fictional story, fungi colonize humans, turning them into zombies that bite one another to spread the fungal infection. The plot isn’t totally implausible as there are fungal infections that parasitize insects and animals for their benefit. In fact, the actual fungus from that story, Ophiocordyceps unilateralis, is parasitic in insects. For example, this fungus infects carpenter ants and directs them to leave their colony and move towards high ground by following light. Usually this means that the carpenter ant climbs a tree or otherwise reaches a high point. Then the fungus produces a spore that projects out of the ant’s head, killing the insect and releasing spores. Since the ant is at a high point, the stalk can distribute spores across a broader area, thereby more efficiently promoting fungal spread. Fungi and Cicadas A different type of fungus, Massoospora cicadina attacks cicadas emerging from hibernation and deteriorates their abdomen and butt, leaving a clump of yellow spores in their place (Cooley et al, 2018). The fungus also ramps up the cicada’s sex drive which facilitates the transfer of fungal spores to other cicadas, and the fungal life cycle continues in that manner. Apparently, the yellow rear-end does repel potential cicada suitors! Fungi and Frogs It’s not just cicadas whose libido is ramped up by fungi, but frogs too. The fungus Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis infects multiple types of frogs and increases their likelihood of finding a mate. Infected male common mist (Litoria rheocola) frogs called more for a female mate during the night compared to uninfected frogs. Their efforts paid off, as on average the infected frogs were more successful at finding a mate (Greenspan et al, 2016). Other researchers found that male Japanese tree frogs (Hyla japonica) infected with the same fungus called for a mate more rapidly and produced longer mating calls compared to uninfected frogs. Again, the infected male frog’s enhanced efforts paid off because they more frequently mated compared to their uninfected counterparts (An & Waldman, 2016). For both of these types of frogs, it is thought that enhanced reproduction rates are coupled with increased fungal transmission, which is why the fungus activates these frogs’ nighttime activities. Barnacles and Crabs Our last example is of a barnacle (Sacculina carcina) tricking a female crab into taking care of it as she would her own babies. First, the barnacle finds the soft spot near joints on the underbelly of a crab where she normally carries her babies. The barnacle then sheds her shell and burrows into the crab, projecting tendrils throughout the crab to steal nutrients and deliver commands to the pseudo-maternal caretaker.
